claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. The first thing I need to know is about the child you're claiming for.

**How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children, we can go through each one separately.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Wonderful! Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 8 years old, they fall into the **under 16** category, which is great news for eligibility purposes.

Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's work status.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, I would expect the answer is **yes** (not working or working very minimal hours), but please confirm this for me.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't work at all - he's only 8! He's still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - Alex is just a young child in primary school. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in his own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by Alex himself. For an 8-year-old, this would almost certainly be **no** - children this young don't typically receive such benefits in their own name.

Can you confirm that Alex is not receiving any of these benefits himself?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in his own name. He's just a kid!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly, that's what I'd expect for an 8-year-old! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

Given that Alex is 8 years old and in primary school, I'm confident the answer is **no** - apprenticeships are for older teenagers and young adults. But please confirm this for me.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! He's far too young for that - he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check about your residency status. 

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through **any one** of the following:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident of the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, it's simply that they **normally live in the UK**.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in the UK meets the residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, Manchester counts! Now I need to check about your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify through **any one** of these:

1. **Living with Alex** - Alex lives with you
2. **Contributing to Alex's upkeep** - You pay at least as much as the Child Benefit amount towards his care (e.g., food, clothes, pocket money, housing)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! That confirms your responsibility for Alex. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, does another parent, guardian, or family member receive Child Benefit for him? Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any time.

- **No one else is claiming** for Alex
- **Someone else claims, but we've agreed I should claim instead**
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's living situation.

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- **Normal living** - Alex lives at home with you in a typical home environment
- **In hospital** - Alex is currently staying in hospital
- **In care** - Alex is in local authority care
- **Fostered** - Alex is in a fostering arrangement

Which of these best describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex has been in hospital for a while now - a couple of months or so.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Alex has been in hospital. I hope he recovers well.

Since Alex has been in hospital for a while, I need to check the duration more specifically as there are rules around this.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I need to understand the hospital stay a bit better.

**How long has Alex been in hospital in total?**

The rules are:
- Child Benefit continues for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- If Alex has had multiple hospital stays with gaps of **less than 28 days** between them, those stays are added together

You mentioned "a couple of months" - could you give me a more specific timeframe? For example:
- **Less than 12 weeks** (under about 3 months)
- **12 weeks or more** (3 months or longer)

Also, has this been one continuous stay, or have there been multiple stays?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been one continuous stay - about 11 weeks in total.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. 11 weeks is under the 12-week limit, which is good news for your Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about your spending on Alex while he's in hospital.

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or books for him
- Bringing food or treats
- Paying for any items he needs during his stay
- Any other regular contributions to his care or comfort

If you have a partner (spouse or civil partner) living with you, their spending also counts.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy things for him - clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks, things like that to keep him comfortable.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's lovely that you're keeping Alex comfortable during his hospital stay. That counts as regularly spending money on him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're nearly at the end of the assessment! Just one more question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex's hospital stay being funded through the NHS or your own means, rather than the local council paying for his care or upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything - it's just the NHS hospital care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That means the council isn't involved in paying for Alex's care - it's standard NHS treatment. Let me complete the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! 🎉

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Good News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex! ✅

Based on our assessment, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

### Summary:
- ✅ Alex is under 16 (8 years old)
- ✅ Alex is not working
- ✅ Alex is not receiving benefits in his own name
- ✅ You live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✅ Alex lives with you (you're responsible for him)
- ✅ No one else is claiming for Alex
- ✅ Alex's hospital stay (11 weeks) is within the 12-week limit
- ✅ You're regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital
- ✅ The local council is not paying for his care

### Next Steps to Claim:
1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note:
If you or your partner earns over **£60,000**, you may be subject to the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through your tax return.

👉 You can apply here: [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

I hope Alex recovers soon! Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old (8 years old), which qualifies him as a child for benefit purposes. (2) He is not working (in primary school). (3) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in an apprenticeship. (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is under the 12-week limit, and the claimant is regularly spending money on him during his stay. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 11 weeks, this is within the 12-week limit, and the claimant continues to regularly spend money on him. All other standard criteria are met - UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no competing claims or council payments.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old (8 years old), which qualifies him as a child for benefit purposes. (2) He is not working (in primary school). (3) He is not receiving any benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in an apprenticeship. (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is under the 12-week limit, and the claimant is regularly spending money on him during his stay. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 11 weeks, this is within the 12-week limit, and the claimant continues to regularly spend money on him. All other standard criteria are met - UK residency, responsibility for the child, and no competing claims or council payments.
